# Quiz ↔ Lecture-Transcript Alignment Review

*Generated by comparing each module's self-check questions against the auto-caption transcripts of its lecture videos (Modules 1–10). Modules 11–12 are slide-only — no transcripts to compare.*

## Method & caveat

Transcripts cover **only the videos**. The quizzes (drawn from the LaTeX quiz bank) test the **whole module** — video **plus** the assigned *Dive into Deep Learning* (D2L) reading **plus** slides. So before calling anything 'misaligned', each apparent gap was cross-checked against the module's assigned D2L sections.

## Verdict

**Strong alignment overall.** Of 48 self-check questions, 38 are squarely covered by the video, 6 are partially covered (the lecture states the result but not the full derivation the answer gives), and 4 aren't in the video. After cross-checking the D2L readings, **only 2 of 48** test material that is in *neither* the video *nor* the module's assigned reading. No keyed answer was found to **contradict** a lecture — the flags are all 'lecture is silent / taught elsewhere', never 'lecture says the opposite'.

| Module | Alignment | Per-question coverage | Answer consistent? |
|---|---|---|---|
| M1 | moderate | ✓ ~ ✓ ✓ ✗ | ✓ ? ✓ ✓ ✗ |
| M2 | moderate | ✓ ✓ ✓ ✓ ✗ | ✓ ✓ ✓ ✓ ? |
| M3 | moderate | ✓ ✓ ✗ ✓ ~ | ✓ ✓ ? ✓ ✓ |
| M4 | strong | ✓ ✓ ✓ ~ | ✓ ✓ ✓ ✓ |
| M5 | strong | ✓ ✓ ~ ✓ | ✓ ✓ ✓ ✓ |

<sub>✓ covered · ~ partial · ✗ not in video · (answer) ✓ confirmed · ? lecture silent · ✗ contradicted</sub>

## The 2 genuine misalignments (worth fixing)

- **M2 Q5** — Softmax + cross-entropy gradient — Module 2 is linear regression/MSE; softmax is **§4.1**, assigned in **Module 1's** reading, not Module 2's. Belongs in M1.
- **M3 Q3** — Training-vs-inference memory (storing activations for backprop) — not in the M3 video and not in M3's reading (§3.6–3.7, Ch.12, Ch.19). Tangential.

## Explained by the assigned reading / adjacent module (no fix needed, optionally add a note)

- **M1 Q2** — Softmax — light in the video, but assigned reading **§4.1 Softmax Regression** covers it fully.
- **M1 Q5** — Universal Approximation — not in the video, but assigned reading **§5.1 MLP** has the universal-approximator result.
- **M8 Q3** — Causal masking — slightly ahead of where it's formally taught (Module 9 / D2L self-attention §11.6–7).
- **M9 Q2** — √d_k scaling — not re-derived in the M9 video, but it's taught in **Module 8's** attention lecture and is in Ch. 11.
- **M10 Q4** — T5 task prefixes — the text-to-text *principle* is taught; the specific 'Summarize:' prefix examples in the answer aren't. Wording nuance.

## Bigger opportunity: lecture topics taught at length but never quizzed

The quizzes are *correct* but *thin* — 4–5 questions can't cover a rich lecture. The highest-value additions, per module:

- **M1** — Stochastic Gradient Descent; Learning rate and batch size trade-offs for convergence and generalization; Momentum and adaptive optimizers
- **M2** — Vanishing and exploding gradients; Batch Normalization; Weight Initialization strategies
- **M3** — Regularization techniques; Early stopping and validation-set methodology for model selection; Batch normalization and layer normalization as critical training techniques
- **M4** — Weight sharing and spatial parameter efficiency as a core inductive bias enabling CNN effectiveness; Translation equivariance vs translation invariance distinction and how convolution achieves the former while pooling adds the latter; Multi-channel convolution mechanics
- **M5** — Inception/GoogLeNet architecture with multi-scale parallel pathways and concatenation strategy; Depthwise separable convolutions and MobileNets for parameter reduction; 1×1 convolutions and Network in Network
- **M6** — Convolutional autoencoders for image compression using conv and transposed-conv layers; Nonlinear autoencoders' ability to learn curved manifolds vs linear autoencoders; Downstream applications of learned representations
- **M7** — Truncated Backpropagation Through Time; Padding and masking strategies; Weight initialization techniques
- **M8** — Additive; Kernel regression interpretation of attention; Layer normalization effect on attention
- **M9** — Causal masking in decoder and prevention of future-token access during training/inference; Multi-head attention mechanism and how subspaced embeddings solve information averaging dilution; Layer normalization and residual skip connections for training stability
- **M10** — Knowledge distillation in Vision Transformers; Chinchilla compute-optimal scaling and the token-to-parameter scaling ratio; In-context learning and few-shot prompting

### Most striking gaps
- **M2 & M3** spend large blocks on vanishing/exploding gradients, ReLU, batch norm, weight initialization, dropout, and residual connections — almost none of which is quizzed (M3's own title is *Ablation Methodology*, yet no question asks students to design an ablation).
- **M10** spends ~30% of lecture 10.1 on **knowledge distillation** and a whole video (10.3) on **Chinchilla / compute-optimal scaling** — neither is quizzed.
